title: Validation of Icing Certification Methods for SMR and Regional Aircraft

Validation of Icing Certification Methods for SMR and Regional Aircraft
Validation of Icing Certification Methods for SMR and Regional Aircraft
The European Commission has initiated a call for proposals aimed at advancing certification methodologies related to aircraft icing, specifically targeting Small Modular Regional (SMR) and regional aircraft. This initiative seeks to foster the development of safer and more efficient certification processes, with the goal of supporting aircraft readiness for entry into service by 2035. Projects may receive up to EUR 12 million in funding over a maximum period of 36 months under the Innovation Action framework, with submissions due by 19 May 2026.
Objectives and Scope
The Commission’s call emphasizes the development and validation of advanced tools and methodologies to improve icing certification. Proposals are expected to focus on creating validated numerical, physics-based, and AI-assisted icing prediction models. These tools should incorporate realistic icing scenarios and detailed droplet physics to enhance prediction accuracy. Additionally, projects must generate comprehensive validation datasets that capture ice accretion and its aerodynamic effects, utilizing wind tunnel testing, experimental data, and existing flight test records. It is important to note that new flight testing will not be eligible for funding.
A further objective is the delivery of draft Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) for Appendix O icing conditions, alongside the establishment of regulatory roadmaps and certification methodologies. The initiative also aims to strengthen European industrial and research capabilities in the field of icing certification, ensuring that the continent remains at the forefront of aviation safety and innovation.
Industry Context and Challenges
The validation of icing certification methods for SMR and regional aircraft presents considerable challenges, particularly in navigating the complex regulatory landscape that includes both Russian and international aviation standards. Recent industry developments, such as United Aircraft’s successful completion of icing tests for new models, illustrate the intricate process of meeting diverse regulatory requirements. This environment is expected to prompt increased scrutiny from aviation regulators and airlines, underscoring the critical importance of robust safety assurances in icing conditions.
Competition within the sector is intensifying, with companies like Bell conducting rigorous testing programs to certify the 525 helicopter, while others, such as Sikorsky, have encountered delays due to missed regulatory milestones. These examples highlight the potential for setbacks in the validation process, including delays linked to evolving certification standards and regulatory timelines, which manufacturers must carefully manage.
Funding, Eligibility, and Application Process
The maximum European Union contribution per project is EUR 12 million, with a project duration capped at 36 months. At least 15% of the total EU funding must be allocated to eligible Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs), Research and Technology Organisations (RTOs), or universities. The call is open to legal entities from EU member states, associated countries, and non-associated third countries, including international organizations. Eligible participants must possess recognized legal personality or equivalent capacity and must register in the Participant Register to obtain a Participant Identification Code (PIC) and complete validation procedures. Associated partners may participate without signing the grant agreement or claiming funding. Entities lacking legal personality may also participate if their representatives assume legal responsibilities and provide necessary financial guarantees.
Interested organizations are advised to verify eligibility for themselves and their consortium partners before registering in the Participant Register to obtain a PIC. Proposals should comprehensively detail the development of numerical and AI-assisted tools, validation datasets and methodologies, and plans for drafting AMC documentation.
Expected Outcomes
The European Commission anticipates that projects funded under this call will significantly reduce the time and cost associated with compliance, streamline certification processes, and enhance the readiness of innovative aircraft technologies for safe entry into service by 2035. In light of increasing regulatory scrutiny and a competitive market landscape, the successful validation of icing certification methods will be essential for manufacturers seeking to introduce new SMR and regional aircraft to the global market.
